topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom lights felt too bright, a relentless shimmer against my eyelids. Every congratulatory hug felt like an obligation I couldn't quite meet. I leaned against a marble pillar, letting the surrounding noise wash over me, a dull, persistent hum. Then, across the crowded dance floor, I saw him. He was laughing, a bright, unguarded sound that snagged my attention like a loose thread. My shoulders slumped infinitesimally. A familiar wave of something heavy settled in my chest, making the effort of staying upright feel monumental. I took a slow sip of champagne, the bubbles tasting flat, and decided the best course of action was to simply observe from the periphery, hoping the current would eventually sweep me away.

[story 2]

I navigated the periphery with practiced indifference, my gaze deliberately fixed on the intricate floral arrangements. The music swelled, demanding participation, and I found myself lagging behind, my movements sluggish. When I finally turned my head, the air seemed to thin. There he was, standing near the buffet, looking impossibly composed amidst the celebratory chaos. A sudden, sharp clench in my jaw was the only outward sign of the internal drain. It was a moment of unwelcome recognition, a brief flicker of old history that left a residue of weariness clinging to my skin. I quickly looked down at my drink, needing the solid anchor of the glass in my hand.

[story 3]

The sheer volume of people was overwhelming; the forced cheerfulness of the occasion grated on my nerves. My feet felt heavy, reluctant to take another step across the polished floor. I kept my pace slow, conserving energy, letting the ambient warmth of the room seep into my extremities. Then, my eyes caught him. He was talking animatedly with someone near the entrance, his expression open and relaxed. A strange, unwelcome slowing occurred in my own rhythm. I managed a small, tight smile—a gesture that felt more like a physical strain than genuine amusement—before deliberately diverting my attention back to the ceiling, wishing for a quiet corner to simply cease existing.
